h1Title: Thoracic Aortic Dissection: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Thoracic aortic dissection occurs when a tear in the inner lining (intima) of the aorta allows blood to surge between the layers of the aortic wall, creating a false channel that can propagate throughout the thoracic and abdominal aorta within seconds to minutes. Without emergent surgery or intensive blood-pressure control, mortality rises by approximately 1-2% per hour in the first 24-48 hours. Annual US incidence is estimated at 3-4 per 100,000 people; men are affected 2-3 times more often than women. The classic presentation is sudden, tearing or ripping chest or back pain of maximal intensity at onset. Type A dissections (involving the ascending aorta) require emergency cardiac surgery; Type B dissections (confined to the descending aorta) are managed medically unless complications develop.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence approximately 3-4 per 100,000 population in Western countries; estimated 10,000-15,000 new cases per year in the United States (IRAD registry)
keyStats.demographics: Men account for 65-70% of cases. Peak incidence in the sixth and seventh decades for degenerative disease; Marfan syndrome and bicuspid aortic valve present in the third and fourth decades.
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at presentation 63-67 years in large registries (IRAD); younger patients (under 40) more often have an underlying connective tissue disorder
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global incidence is unknown due to misdiagnosis and out-of-hospital deaths; autopsy series suggest up to 30% of acute dissections are not diagnosed before death

definition: Thoracic aortic dissection (ICD-10: I71.0) is a life-threatening condition in which a longitudinal tear in the aortic intima creates a communication between the true lumen and a dissecting haematoma that propagates within the media. The process begins with an intimal tear driven by haemodynamic stress — usually at the right lateral wall of the ascending aorta, just above the aortic valve — where shear forces are greatest, or at the proximal descending aorta just distal to the left subclavian artery, a site of ligamentum arteriosum tethering. The dissection flap creates two parallel flow channels (true and false lumen); the false lumen may compress or occlude branch vessels including the coronary, carotid, subclavian, mesenteric, renal, and iliac arteries, causing malperfusion syndromes. The Stanford classification (A and B) and DeBakey classification (I, II, III) guide operative decision-making. Stanford Type A involves the ascending aorta and requires emergency surgery; Stanford Type B is confined to the descending aorta and is initially managed with anti-impulse therapy unless complicated by malperfusion, rapid expansion, or persistent pain. Cardiothoracic and vascular surgeons manage this condition, with increasingly frequent use of thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) for selected Type B cases.

typesClassification.type: Stanford Type A (DeBakey I and II)
typesClassification.description: Involves the ascending aorta, with or without the arch and descending aorta. Accounts for 60-70% of acute dissections. Carries 1-2% per hour mortality without surgery from aortic regurgitation, coronary ostial involvement, cardiac tamponade, and stroke. Requires emergent surgical replacement of the ascending aorta.

Confined to the descending thoracic aorta, beginning distal to the left subclavian artery. Accounts for 30-40% of cases. Uncomplicated Type B is managed with anti-impulse therapy (IV beta-blockers targeting heart rate <60 and SBP 100-120 mmHg). Complicated Type B — with malperfusion, rupture, or expansion — is treated with TEVAR.

Defined by any of: ischaemia of the spinal cord, viscera, kidneys, or limbs; contained aortic rupture; refractory hypertension; or rapid aortic expansion. TEVAR seals the primary entry tear and re-establishes true-lumen flow; 30-day mortality is 8-15% in experienced centres.

Dissection diagnosed more than 2 weeks after onset, often detected incidentally. The false lumen may thrombose (better prognosis) or remain patent with progressive aneurysmal dilatation. Long-term surveillance CT every 6-12 months is mandatory; elective repair is considered when the aortic diameter exceeds 5.5 cm.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, severe, tearing or ripping pain in the chest, back between the shoulder blades, or both simultaneously — the single most important discriminating symptom, reaching maximum intensity at onset in over 80% of patients (IRAD data).
Pain that migrates from the anterior chest toward the back, abdomen, or flanks as the dissection propagates distally along the aorta.
Pulse differential or pulse deficit — absent or reduced radial, femoral, or carotid pulse on one side compared with the other, resulting from dissection flap occluding a branch vessel.
Blood pressure differential — systolic BP difference exceeding 20 mmHg between the two arms, a specific but insensitive sign occurring in approximately 25% of cases.
Aortic regurgitation murmur and signs of acute heart failure (pulmonary oedema, dyspnoea) when the dissection extends retrogradely to involve the aortic root and valve.
Neurological deficits — sudden hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, or reduced consciousness from carotid or vertebral artery occlusion; paraplegia from spinal artery compromise.
Syncope at the onset of pain — reported in 10-15% of patients; may result from cardiac tamponade, haemodynamic instability, or cerebral malperfusion.
Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or bloody diarrhoea from mesenteric artery malperfusion; anuria or haematuria from renal artery involvement.
Lower limb ischaemia — cold, pale, pulseless legs from iliac artery occlusion by the dissection flap.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset of severe interscapular or chest pain in a hypertensive patient — any maximum-intensity pain at onset should raise suspicion for dissection before MI.

Known diagnosis of Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve, or family history of aortic aneurysm — these patients should have a low threshold for aortic imaging with any chest pain.
Unexplained new pulse deficit or arm blood pressure asymmetry on routine examination in a hypertensive patient.
Prior imaging showing ascending aortic diameter above 4.5 cm, a threshold above which dissection risk begins to rise significantly.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe chest or back pain of maximal intensity at onset — call emergency services immediately; do not attempt to drive; delay to diagnosis directly worsens outcomes.
Syncope combined with chest pain — high suspicion for cardiac tamponade from haemopericardium; urgent pericardiocentesis or surgical decompression may be required.
Acute limb or neurological ischaemia with chest pain — indicates malperfusion and changes management urgently from blood-pressure control alone to emergency revascularisation or surgery.
Haemodynamic collapse (SBP < 90 mmHg, shock, altered consciousness) — suspect Type A dissection with rupture; immediate surgical consult required.
New aortic regurgitation murmur in the context of acute chest pain — implies Type A dissection with aortic root involvement; surgical priority.

causes.cause: Chronic hypertension — the dominant cause in over two-thirds of cases
causes.description: Sustained elevated wall stress (LaPlace: stress = pressure × radius / thickness) fatigues the medial elastic fibres of the aortic wall. Hypertension is present in 65-75% of acute dissection patients at presentation; poorly controlled hypertension accelerates medial degeneration and dramatically increases dissection risk.

Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation) causes cystic medial necrosis with fragmented elastic fibres, conferring a 40% lifetime risk of aortic dissection; elective surgery is recommended when ascending diameter reaches 5.0 cm. Loeys-Dietz syndrome (TGFBR1/2 mutations) is even more aggressive, with dissection occurring at smaller diameters.

Present in 1-2% of the population, bicuspid aortic valve is associated with aortopathy — intrinsic medial weakness of the ascending aorta from abnormal haemodynamics and NOTCH1-related signalling. Ascending aortic diameter above 4.5 cm warrants closer surveillance; surgery at 5.0-5.5 cm depending on risk factors.

Cardiac catheterisation, intra-aortic balloon pump insertion, aortic cross-clamping during cardiac surgery, and cannulation during cardiopulmonary bypass all carry small risks of iatrogenic dissection. These account for approximately 5% of all dissections and are classified as Type A or B depending on the site of injury.

Cocaine causes acute sympathomimetic surges — heart rate and blood pressure spikes — that can propagate a tear in a pre-existing vulnerable aorta. Cocaine-related dissection accounts for 2-5% of cases in urban emergency registries and presents in younger patients, often with minimal traditional risk factors.

High-energy thoracic trauma (motor vehicle collision, fall from height) can shear the aorta at sites of relative fixation — typically the aortic isthmus just distal to the left subclavian artery. These traumatic aortic injuries are classified separately from classic dissection but share the same operative platform (TEVAR).

riskFactors.description: Present in 65-75% of dissection patients at presentation. Blood pressure above 160/100 mmHg at least doubles aortic wall stress. Achieving target SBP below 120 mmHg is the primary modifiable risk reduction strategy.
Men have 2-3× higher incidence than women across all age groups; partly explained by higher rates of aortic aneurysm, and possibly hormonal differences in medial composition.
Medial degeneration accelerates with aging; aortic stiffness and diameter both increase linearly with age. Incidence per 100,000 approximately doubles between ages 50 and 70.

riskFactors.factor: Marfan syndrome or other connective tissue disorder
riskFactors.description: Lifetime dissection risk of 40-50% in untreated Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutations). Loeys-Dietz, Ehlers-Danlos vascular type, and Turner syndrome each carry elevated aortic risk requiring dedicated surveillance.
Affects 1-2% of the population; associated with ascending aortic dilatation and 4-9-fold higher risk of aortic dissection than the general population.
Sympathomimetic surge causes acute blood pressure spikes sufficient to propagate a tear in a pre-existing weakened aorta; particularly significant in dissection presenting in patients under 40.

Surgical anastomoses, prosthetic grafts, and residual dissection segments from prior repair are predisposed to redissection; lifelong CT surveillance and aggressive blood-pressure management are mandatory.

affectedDemographics: Men aged 60-70 years represent the largest demographic in most registries. Male-to-female ratio approximately 2-3:1 in the sixth and seventh decades.
Younger patients (under 45) disproportionately have Marfan syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve, or cocaine use as the underlying cause.

Women who present with dissection are on average 5-10 years older than men, have a higher proportion of Type A dissection, and have worse in-hospital outcomes in most registries — possibly because diagnosis is delayed.
Patients with a family history of aortic aneurysm have 2-5× higher risk and warrant screening CT at age 40-50.
diagnosisOverview: Acute aortic dissection is a diagnostic emergency where speed directly impacts survival. The Aortic Dissection Detection Risk Score (ADD-RS) and the diagnostic algorithm from the 2014 AHA/ACC guidelines stratify pre-test probability from the initial clinical assessment. A high-risk presentation (maximum-intensity pain at onset, pulse differential, widened mediastinum on chest X-ray) mandates immediate cross-sectional imaging. CT aortography with contrast — covering from the carotid arteries to the femoral vessels — is the diagnostic gold standard, with sensitivity and specificity above 95%. It characterises the entry tear, extent of dissection, involvement of branches, and the presence of malperfusion or rupture. The characteristic finding is a dissection flap separating the true and false lumen, often with differential opacification. ECG and high-sensitivity troponin are essential to simultaneously exclude STEMI (anticoagulation in MI can be catastrophic in dissection). Chest X-ray shows widened mediastinum (above 8 cm) in only 60-70% of cases and is not sensitive enough to exclude dissection. Transoesophageal echocardiography (TOE) is used in haemodynamically unstable patients unsuitable for CT, with sensitivity above 90% for the ascending aorta but limited view of the descending aorta. MRI offers the highest accuracy but is impractical in acute settings. D-dimer below 500 ng/mL may help rule out dissection in very low pre-test probability patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging to confirm dissection, define extent, classify Type A vs B, identify entry tear, and assess branch vessel involvement and complications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 15-20 minutes in the CT scanner; iodinated contrast is injected through a forearm cannula. The scan covers neck to pelvis. Results are available within minutes and must be reviewed immediately by a vascular radiologist. Patients are screened for renal function and contrast allergy beforehand when time permits.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes concurrent STEMI, which requires anticoagulation that is contraindicated in dissection; identifies coronary ostial involvement causing inferior STEMI in Type A
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-minute test with adhesive leads on the chest, arms, and legs. Results interpreted immediately. A normal ECG does not exclude dissection; an inferior STEMI pattern may indicate right coronary artery involvement by the flap.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies mediastinal widening (>8 cm), pleural effusion, and cardiomegaly; rapid but not sensitive enough alone to confirm or exclude dissection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 2 minutes in the radiology department or at the bedside in unstable patients. A normal CXR does not exclude dissection; up to 30% of confirmed cases have a normal initial CXR.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Bedside imaging for unstable patients unsuitable for CT; evaluates aortic root, valve, and proximal ascending aorta; detects pericardial effusion and tamponade
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under light sedation or general anaesthesia by a cardiologist or anaesthesiologist. A flexible probe is passed into the oesophagus. Takes 15-20 minutes. Not suitable if oesophageal disease is present.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates from acute MI and identifies coexistent myocardial ischaemia from coronary ostial involvement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 30-60 minutes. In dissection, troponin may be mildly elevated from demand ischaemia; a massively elevated troponin with matching ECG changes suggests primary coronary occlusion rather than dissection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A normal D-dimer (below 500 ng/mL) can reduce post-test probability in low-risk presentations (ADD-RS score 0); does not definitively exclude dissection at higher risk scores
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 30-60 minutes. Sensitivity of negative D-dimer for dissection is 97% but specificity is low — it is only useful to help rule out dissection in genuinely low-risk patients.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends entirely on the Stanford classification and the presence of complications, following the 2022 ACC/AHA Aortic Disease Guideline. Stanford Type A dissection is a surgical emergency: untreated, 25% die within 24 hours and 50% by 48 hours from cardiac tamponade, aortic regurgitation, or coronary malperfusion. Emergency ascending aortic replacement, including resuspension or replacement of the aortic valve and reimplantation of the coronary arteries if involved, restores true-lumen patency and prevents rupture into the pericardium. In-hospital mortality for Type A repair at experienced centres is 15-25%; the IRAD registry reports 22% overall mortality for Type A, falling to below 15% in high-volume aortic centres. Stanford Type B without complications is managed with anti-impulse therapy: intravenous esmolol or labetalol to achieve a heart rate of 55-65 bpm and a systolic blood pressure of 100-120 mmHg, followed by oral beta-blockade. This targets the pulsatile aortic wall stress that propagates the dissection. Complicated Type B — with malperfusion, rupture, refractory hypertension, or aortic expansion above 5.5 cm — is treated with thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR), in which a stent-graft is deployed over the primary entry tear via a femoral artery catheter under fluoroscopic guidance. TEVAR seals the false lumen and redirects flow to the true lumen, relieving branch vessel compression. Sub-acute and chronic Type B with false-lumen patency require lifelong CT surveillance at 1, 3, and 6 months and annually thereafter, targeting SBP below 120 mmHg indefinitely.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-impulse therapy — intravenous esmolol (50-200 µg/kg/min) or labetalol (20-80 mg IV boluses)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line haemodynamic control targeting HR 55-65 bpm and SBP 100-120 mmHg to reduce aortic wall shear stress and halt dissection propagation. Esmolol is preferred for its titratable ultra-short action (9-minute half-life). Sodium nitroprusside is added if BP remains elevated after adequate beta-blockade but should never be used without prior beta-blockade (reflex tachycardia worsens dissection).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Anti-impulse therapy reduces 30-day mortality in uncomplicated Type B to approximately 10-13% (IRAD 2004 data); in-hospital Type B mortality below 10% with modern protocols

medicalTreatments.name: Oral beta-blockers for long-term aortic impulse control (metoprolol succinate, atenolol, carvedilol)
medicalTreatments.description: Transition from IV to oral anti-impulse therapy once haemodynamics are stable; continued for life in all dissection patients regardless of subsequent intervention. Target resting heart rate 60-70 bpm; SBP below 120 mmHg; may combine with ACE inhibitor or calcium channel blocker for BP control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Long-term beta-blockade associated with lower aortic growth rate (0.1-0.3 cm/year vs 0.5-0.8 cm/year without treatment) in retrospective series

medicalTreatments.name: Pain management — intravenous morphine or fentanyl
medicalTreatments.description: Adequate analgesia reduces catecholamine release, which otherwise drives tachycardia and hypertension, worsening the haemodynamic environment. Titrate IV opioids to pain relief without excessive sedation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Not independently trialled as an outcome modifier; essential component of haemodynamic stabilisation alongside beta-blockade

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors or ARBs for long-term blood pressure control
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line antihypertensives after beta-blockade for both Type A post-surgery and Type B; benefit supported for Marfan syndrome patients where losartan may slow aortic root dilatation beyond blood-pressure effects (COMPARE trial).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Blood pressure reduction below 120/80 mmHg reduces aortic event rate by 30-40% in Marfan long-term follow-up studies

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency ascending aortic replacement (Type A open surgery)
surgicalOptions.description: The ascending aorta from the sinotubular junction to the arch is replaced with a prosthetic Dacron graft under cardiopulmonary bypass and deep hypothermic circulatory arrest. The aortic valve is resuspended (re-suspension repair) or replaced if torn or incompetent. When the coronary ostia are involved, they are reimplanted (Bentall procedure).
surgicalOptions.successRate: In-hospital mortality 15-25% overall in IRAD; falls to below 10-12% in centres performing more than 50 annual Type A repairs. 5-year survival after hospital discharge approximately 75-80%.

surgicalOptions.name: Total aortic arch replacement with elephant trunk technique
surgicalOptions.description: When the dissection involves the arch and descending aorta, the arch vessels are reimplanted as an island or individually, and a trunk of prosthetic graft is left in the descending aorta to facilitate a second-stage procedure (frozen elephant trunk or TEVAR completion).
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day mortality 5-15% at high-volume centres; neurological morbidity (stroke, paraplegia) 5-10%.

surgicalOptions.name: Thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) for complicated Type B
surgicalOptions.description: A covered stent-graft is deployed from the femoral artery under fluoroscopic guidance to seal the primary entry tear in the descending thoracic aorta. TEVAR reduces false-lumen pressurisation, promotes false-lumen thrombosis, and relieves malperfusion. Spinal drainage is used prophylactically when extensive coverage is planned to prevent paraplegia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success above 95%; 30-day mortality 8-12% for complicated Type B in registries. False-lumen thrombosis achieved in 80-90% of treated segments at 2 years in INSTEAD-XL trial (PMID 23583244).

alternativeTreatments.name: Fenestration (catheter-based aortic fenestration)
alternativeTreatments.description: A catheter technique that creates communications between true and false lumen to relieve malperfusion when TEVAR is not feasible or malperfusion persists after TEVAR. Used specifically for limb or visceral ischaemia.

whySeeSpecialist: Any suspected aortic dissection requires immediate emergency department evaluation and a cardiothoracic or vascular surgical team. Elective referral to a specialist aortic centre is indicated for patients with known aortic aneurysm greater than 4.5 cm, Marfan or Loeys-Dietz syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve with ascending dilatation, or a family history of aortic events — to establish a surveillance and pre-emptive surgical plan before dissection occurs.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute dissection, management at a high-volume aortic centre is strongly associated with survival benefit: centres performing more than 50 Type A repairs annually have mortality rates 30-40% lower than low-volume centres. For chronic surveillance and elective repair, seek a multidisciplinary aortic programme with dedicated cardiothoracic surgery, vascular surgery, cardiac imaging, and a connective tissue disorder clinic.
hospitalCriteria: 24/7 cardiac surgery capability for emergency Type A repair
Dedicated aortic programme or heart team with >50 annual aortic cases
Hybrid operating theatre for TEVAR in complicated Type B
Advanced cardiac intensive care unit with intra-aortic balloon pump and ECMO
Connective tissue disease clinic for Marfan and Loeys-Dietz surveillance
keyFacilities: High-volume aortic surgical centres (>50 annual Type A repairs)
preventionStrategies: Achieve blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg (and ideally below 120/80 mmHg in Marfan or bicuspid aortic valve patients) through lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.
Avoid cocaine, methamphetamine, and other stimulants — implicated in up to 5% of dissections in urban populations.
Screen first-degree relatives of patients with aortic aneurysm or dissection with CT or MRI aortography at age 40-50.

Initiate elective repair before dissection in high-risk patients: ascending aorta ≥5.5 cm in the general population, ≥5.0 cm in Marfan syndrome, ≥4.5 cm in bicuspid aortic valve with additional risk factors.
Avoid heavy isometric exercise (weightlifting, intense resistance training) and competitive contact sports in patients with known aortic dilatation above 4.5 cm.
lifestyleModifications: Take anti-impulse medications (beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors) every day without interruption regardless of feeling well — blood pressure surges precipitate extension of residual dissection.
Monitor home blood pressure daily; maintain SBP below 120 mmHg at rest.
Attend scheduled CT surveillance imaging at 1, 3, and 6 months in the first year then annually — aortic growth above 0.5 cm/year warrants reassessment for intervention.
Avoid isometric and Valsalva-type exercise (heavy lifting, straining); safe alternatives include walking, cycling, and swimming at moderate intensity.
Carry a card or wear a medical-alert bracelet documenting the history of aortic dissection for emergency providers.
Inform all healthcare providers — including dentists and anaesthesiologists — of aortic history before procedures, as instrumentation can cause haemodynamic stress.

recommended: Low-sodium diet (below 2,300 mg/day) to support blood pressure control alongside antihypertensive medication
DASH dietary pattern rich in fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy, and whole grains — reduces systolic BP by 8-11 mmHg
Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) for anti-inflammatory and mild antihypertensive effects
Adequate potassium intake from vegetables and legumes to counteract sodium-mediated BP elevation
avoid: High-sodium processed foods, restaurant meals, cured meats — each 1 g/day sodium reduction lowers SBP by approximately 2 mmHg
Excessive caffeine in large boluses (e.g. energy drinks) which can acutely elevate blood pressure
Liquorice root (glycyrrhizin) which causes sodium retention and secondary hypertension
Alcohol above 2 standard drinks per day — raises blood pressure and increases haemodynamic variability

exerciseGuidelines: After uncomplicated Type B dissection managed medically: gentle aerobic activity (walking, light cycling) is encouraged once haemodynamics are stable at 4-6 weeks. Avoid heavy resistance training, contact sports, or intense exertion indefinitely when aortic diameter exceeds 4.5 cm. After Type A surgical repair: sternal precautions for 6-8 weeks, then graduated cardiac rehabilitation with cardiologist and surgeon approval. All post-dissection patients should avoid competitive contact sports and isometric Valsalva-intensive activities.
dailyManagement: Take beta-blocker (and ACE inhibitor or ARB if prescribed) at the same time each day; never stop abruptly without physician guidance.
Measure and record home blood pressure and resting heart rate morning and evening; target SBP 100-120 mmHg, HR 55-65 bpm.
Attend all scheduled CT imaging appointments — aortic growth is silent and can only be detected by serial imaging.
Report any new chest, back, or abdominal pain immediately to emergency services — do not wait for the next appointment.
Inform emergency departments that you have a history of aortic dissection before receiving any anticoagulants, thrombolytics, or aggressive fluid resuscitation.
Keep a medication and imaging log to share with any new provider.
prognosis: Prognosis differs dramatically by type and speed of treatment. Type A dissection treated with emergency surgery has an in-hospital survival of 75-85% in high-volume aortic centres; 5-year survival after hospital discharge is approximately 75-80%, and 10-year survival is 50-60%, with most late deaths from aortic complications in the residual dissected segments. Untreated Type A carries near-certain death within days. Uncomplicated Type B has an in-hospital survival above 90% with anti-impulse therapy, but 20-30% require intervention within 5 years for false-lumen expansion, and 5-year overall survival is approximately 80-85%. Patients with Marfan syndrome who undergo root replacement and remain on lifelong beta-blockade have survival approaching age-matched non-Marfan populations. Predictors of poor outcome include shock at presentation, malperfusion, older age, and renal failure. Long-term CT surveillance and strict blood-pressure control are the foundations of post-dissection management.
recoveryTimeline: After Type A surgery: intensive care 2-5 days; hospital discharge 7-14 days; sternal healing 6-8 weeks; return to light activity 8-12 weeks; full functional recovery 3-6 months. After TEVAR for Type B: intensive care 1-2 days; hospital discharge 4-7 days; return to light activity in 4-6 weeks. Lifelong CT surveillance every 6-12 months is mandatory for all dissection survivors regardless of treatment modality.
complications: False-lumen aneurysmal dilatation requiring delayed TEVAR or open repair — occurs in 20-40% of Type B patients with a patent false lumen within 5 years; detected by serial CT.

Spinal cord ischaemia and paraplegia — complicates 3-10% of TEVAR procedures involving extensive thoracic coverage; prophylactic spinal drainage and permissive hypertension post-operatively reduce risk.
Stroke — from carotid or arch vessel involvement in Type A, or from manipulation of an atherosclerotic arch during TEVAR; documented in 5-10% of Type A repairs.
Redissection at anastomotic sites or in residual dissected segments — requires lifelong CT surveillance and aggressive BP management.
Renal failure — from pre-operative renal malperfusion or peri-operative haemodynamic instability; dialysis required in 10-15% of post-operative Type A patients.
Aortic valve regurgitation in residual or recurrent Type A — may require re-operative root replacement; echocardiographic surveillance at 6-12 months after initial repair.

supportResources.name: Aortic Dissection Awareness UK
supportResources.description: Patient charity providing information, survivor stories, and awareness resources for patients and families affected by aortic dissection.

supportResources.name: Marfan Foundation — Aortic Disease
supportResources.description: Comprehensive resources for patients with Marfan syndrome and hereditary aortopathies, including aortic surveillance guidance and surgical decision tools.

supportResources.name: Society for Vascular Surgery — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: US-based patient information on aortic aneurysm and dissection management, choosing an aortic centre, and endovascular options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Coronary Artery Disease / Acute MI
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause severe chest pain but the onset pattern is the key discriminator: dissection pain is maximal at onset and often tearing; MI pain builds over minutes. MI is associated with ST-elevation on ECG, a rising troponin, and relief from nitrates. Critically, thrombolytics given for a misdiagnosed dissection can be fatal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic Ulcer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcer causes epigastric pain relieved by food or antacids, without chest tearing quality, pulse differential, or mediastinal widening. However, a posterior aortic dissection extending to the abdominal aorta can mimic severe abdominal pain; CT aortography differentiates the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary Embolism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pulmonary embolism causes pleuritic chest pain, dyspnoea, and hypoxia with raised D-dimer and CT pulmonary angiography showing filling defects in pulmonary arteries. Aortic dissection causes tearing pain with pulse differential and a dissection flap on CT aortography. Heparinisation safe for PE is dangerous in dissection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Musculoskeletal Back Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Musculoskeletal back pain is positional, worse with movement, and lacks the abrupt maximal-intensity onset of dissection. Dissection back pain is typically between the shoulder blades, accompanies anterior chest pain, and is not reproduced by palpation or movement.

relatedConditions.name: Peripheral Artery Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Dissection of the abdominal aorta and iliac vessels can cause acute limb ischaemia; patients with aortic disease frequently have concurrent peripheral atherosclerosis.

relatedConditions.name: Coronary Artery Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares hypertension and atherosclerosis as dominant risk factors; coronary ostial involvement in Type A dissection causes acute MI as a complication.

relatedConditions.name: Heart Failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe aortic regurgitation from Type A dissection causes acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema and left ventricular failure requiring emergency repair.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Carotid and vertebral artery involvement in Type A dissection causes ischaemic stroke in 5-10% of cases; a co-presenting neurological deficit changes surgical priority.

faqs.question: What does aortic dissection feel like?
faqs.answer: Aortic dissection typically causes sudden, severe, tearing or ripping pain in the chest or between the shoulder blades that is maximal in intensity at onset — unlike a heart attack where pain builds gradually. The pain may move toward the abdomen as the tear propagates. It is often described as the worst pain ever experienced.

faqs.question: How is aortic dissection diagnosed?
faqs.answer: CT aortography with contrast is the gold-standard diagnostic test, showing the dissection flap separating true and false lumens. ECG and troponin are done simultaneously to exclude myocardial infarction. In unstable patients, transoesophageal echocardiography can diagnose Type A dissection at the bedside.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Type A and Type B aortic dissection?
faqs.answer: Type A dissection involves the ascending aorta (the section closest to the heart) and is a surgical emergency requiring immediate open-heart surgery; without surgery, mortality is approximately 1-2% per hour. Type B is confined to the descending aorta and is initially managed with blood-pressure-lowering medication unless complications develop.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for aortic dissection?
faqs.answer: With emergency surgery, Type A dissection in-hospital survival is 75-85% at high-volume centres. Uncomplicated Type B treated medically has over 90% in-hospital survival. Without surgery, Type A carries near-certain death within days. Long-term 5-year survival after hospital discharge is 75-80% for both types with ongoing surveillance.

faqs.question: Can aortic dissection be cured?
faqs.answer: Emergency surgery cures the acute Type A crisis by replacing the torn segment, but the remaining aorta retains the dissection and remains at risk for future events. Type B may stabilise without surgery. All survivors require lifelong CT imaging, strict blood pressure control, and regular specialist review.

faqs.question: What causes aortic dissection?
faqs.answer: Chronic hypertension is the dominant cause, present in 65-75% of patients. Other causes include Marfan syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve, cocaine use, and blunt chest trauma. These conditions weaken the aortic wall or cause acute blood pressure surges that propagate a tear through the inner lining.

faqs.question: Is aortic dissection the same as a heart attack?
faqs.answer: No. A heart attack is caused by a blocked coronary artery cutting off blood to the heart muscle. Aortic dissection is a tear in the wall of the aorta itself. Both cause severe chest pain but have different treatments. Anticoagulants and thrombolytics safe in heart attacks can be fatal in aortic dissection.

faqs.question: What are the risk factors for aortic dissection?
faqs.answer: The main risk factors are uncontrolled high blood pressure, Marfan syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve, male sex, age over 60, cocaine use, and a family history of aortic aneurysm. Prior aortic surgery and connective tissue disorders such as Loeys-Dietz syndrome are also significant.

faqs.question: Can aortic dissection happen without symptoms?
faqs.answer: Rarely. Most acute dissections cause severe pain. Chronic dissection discovered on routine imaging can be asymptomatic. However, painless dissection with only neurological symptoms (stroke, paraplegia) is documented and may be misdiagnosed. Any sudden-onset worst-ever pain should prompt urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: How is Type B aortic dissection treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated Type B is treated with anti-impulse therapy: intravenous beta-blockers (esmolol or labetalol) to lower heart rate to 55-65 bpm and systolic BP to 100-120 mmHg, then transitioned to oral beta-blockers for life. This reduces aortic wall stress and halts dissection propagation in most cases.

faqs.question: How long is recovery from aortic dissection surgery?
faqs.answer: After Type A open repair: ICU stay 2-5 days, hospital discharge 7-14 days, sternal healing 6-8 weeks, and full recovery 3-6 months. After TEVAR for Type B: hospital discharge in 4-7 days and return to light activity in 4-6 weeks. All patients require lifelong CT surveillance every 6-12 months.

faqs.question: Can people with Marfan syndrome prevent aortic dissection?
faqs.answer: Yes, substantially. Lifelong beta-blocker therapy (or losartan) slows aortic root dilatation. Elective root replacement surgery at aortic diameter of 5.0 cm (or 4.5 cm with additional risk factors) eliminates most dissection risk in the replaced segment. Annual echocardiography or CT starting at diagnosis is essential.

faqs.question: What is TEVAR and when is it used for aortic dissection?
faqs.answer: Thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) places a stent-graft over the primary tear in the descending aorta via a catheter from the femoral artery, sealing the false lumen. It is used for complicated Type B dissection — when there is malperfusion, rupture, or rapid aortic expansion — and avoids open chest surgery.

faqs.question: What blood pressure is safe after aortic dissection?
faqs.answer: All post-dissection patients should maintain resting systolic blood pressure below 120 mmHg and heart rate 55-70 bpm on lifelong beta-blockade. Home blood pressure monitoring twice daily is recommended. Achieving these targets is the single most important long-term intervention to prevent aortic growth and re-dissection.

faqs.question: Can aortic dissection happen again after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Surgery replaces the ascending aorta but the residual descending aorta retains the dissection and remains at risk of re-dissection or aneurysmal dilatation. Approximately 20-40% of Type A survivors require additional aortic intervention within 10 years. Lifelong CT surveillance at 6-12 month intervals is mandatory.

symptomKeywords: chest pain with blood pressure difference
